← the Atlas

padi — the per-host terminal-workspace daemon

seedling·proposed·JUL 01, 2026

The architecture that replaces the R9/R10 finale plan for remote terminals (#951). Extract kolu-server's entire terminal domain into padi — one workspace daemon per (host, state-root), atop kaval — serving ONE complete surface (padiSurface). kolu-server thins to a web shell holding per-connection host bindings; the canvas is single-host per view (model A+ — instant switch, cross-host badges via OS notifications). The host owns its state — layout, memory, fold, clock. pulam and pulam-web dissolve; PRs

Contents

  • User-facing description
  • Architecture-level changes
    • The three layers, and what each owns
    • The state model — what survives from the awareness design, and what moves
    • Session state and restore — the host restores itself
    • padiSurface — one contract, with per-member forwarding semantics
    • Rejected shapes (each killed by a specific review finding)
  • Implementation details
    • W0 — decks cleared (all three landed)
    • W1 — the padi seam, in place (strangler; eight PRs, phased below; UX byte-identical throughout)
    • W2 — padi the process (local; four PRs, phased below; UX byte-identical; restarts get warm)
    • W3 — remote + the switch (three PRs, phased below; the user-visible feature)
    • W4 — demand-driven
    • Risks, named, with mitigations
    • Superseded by this note

The ground-up re-architecture for #951 — remote terminals — and the extraction that untangles kolu-server and the client along the way. The finale plan (PR-1 · PR-2 · PR-3 · F-REMOTE · R10) threaded a per-terminal HostLocation through every seam of kolu-server because it assumed tiles from several hosts share one canvas. Stepping back dissolved the premise: “which host” is a property of the connection, not of a terminal. One daemon per host — padi (படி, the stepped stand a koḷu is arranged on) — owns everything about that host’s terminals and serves it as one complete surface; kolu-server becomes a thin web shell that binds to a padi per client view. The four prep PRs (#1637 #1638 #1639 #1640) are closed as superseded; pulam and pulam-web dissolve into padi. This design was hardened by a 7-lens adversarial review (state-and-time, Löwy, Hickey, a defect-constructing adversary, feasibility-against-code, an A-vs-B adjudicator, a completeness critic — every finding independently skeptic-verified) plus a persona-driven UX evaluation; the load-bearing outcomes of both are baked in below.

ROADMAP — padi (replaces R9/R10) · kept up to date✓ shipped · ▶ do next · ◐ build-clean · ○ todo · → drill in
✓W0 · decks clearedall landed
✓overflow frame — typed overflow → re-attach on kaval's attach contract#1591 · kaval 5.0
✓#1637–#1640 closed · finale superseded2026-07-01
✓freeze terminalWorkspaceSurface at 3.0 · retire pulam-web#1650
○W1 · the padi seam, in place — strangler inside kolu-server, UX byte-identical8 PRs ↓
○W1.1 · padiSurface 1.0 + control core, served in-process (complete, zero consumers)opens all W1.x
○W1.2 · the composed terminals collection — client join deleted∥ after W1.1
○W1.3 · lifecycle + chrome procedures — root terminal.* starts dying∥ after W1.1
○W1.4 · terminalAttach fail-through stream + screen procedures∥ after W1.1
○W1.5 · Code tab on procedure + pulse — koluSurface value streams deleted∥ after W1.1
○W1.6 · bytes on the surface — preview · scratch · transcript · worktrees∥ after W1.1
○W1.7 · session — parked state · session.restore/import · client loop deletedneeds W1.2 W1.3
○W1.8 · MRU + status relocation, and the import-seallast — needs all W1.x
○W2 · padi the process — 4 PRs ↓ · byte-identical · warm restartsneeds W1
○W2.1 · the re-serve machinery in @kolu/surface* — forwarding policy · per-binding scopedrishti-gated · ∥ with W1
○W2.2 · the padi binary — identity · rendezvous · control core · owns kaval · Nix/staleKeyzero kolu consumers
○W2.3 · kolu-server binds — adopt-or-spawn-or-refuse · re-serve · store migration · e2e rootsneeds W2.1 W2.2
○W2.4 · burial — pulam + pulam-tui retired · wait re-homed · terminalWorkspaceSurface DELETEDneeds W2.3
○W3 · remote + the switch — 3 PRs ↓ · the user-visible featureneeds W2
○W3.1 · the remote binding — provision + front a remote padi (complete, invisible)parity e2e on a pu box
○W3.2 · the switch — binding pool · per-view scope · host picker · clock handshakeneeds W3.1
○W3.3 · cross-host attention — urgency fan-in · OS badge · notification deep-linkneeds W3.2
○W4 · demand-driven — cross-host dock · kolu-tui · hybrid only if earned

User-facing description

hostlocalsshnix@prodsshpu-kolu-32+ host⌥H switch
The whole canvas is nix@prod's — its own layout, its own dock, exactly as any other device sees it. pu-kolu-3 has 2 agents awaiting you: the chip badge, the PWA app badge, and an OS notification all say so — clicking the notification switches the view and focuses the tile (one action).

Nothing changes until W3. Then: the ChromeBar grows a host switcher — kinda like tmux sessions. A canvas shows one host’s terminals; switching hosts swaps the whole canvas instantly (bindings stay warm server-side). A host’s arrangement — tile layout, names, themes, remembered agent commands — lives on that host, so your desktop, laptop, and phone all see the same canvas for nix@prod, and a teammate-free second kolu (or a future kolu-tui) joins the same arrangement. A remote canvas is a full peer of the local one: Code tab, binary preview, paste/upload, transcript export, sleep/wake, session restore — all against the terminal’s own host, nothing silently wrong-host.

While you’re parked on one host, the others reach you through the channels an installed PWA already owns: the app badge aggregates awaiting-agents across all bound hosts, the switcher chips carry per-host counts, and an OS notification’s click is one action — switch binding + focus the tile. (The per-terminal plumbing — setAppBadge, service-worker notifications — already exists; W3 aggregates it across bindings.)

Two personas anchor the design, and both get the same shape: an ephemeral cloud box (a pu-style instance per project — teardown removes the host from the picker as one clean unit, never wreckage on a shared canvas) and a permanent headless server (days-long agents; hop local⇄server many times a day — instant switch + 1-action notification response is the tmux-grade loop that persona lives in).

Why not hybrid tiles (several hosts on one canvas)? Evaluated, deferred — not foreclosed

A persona-driven UX pass compared switch (A), warm-multi-binding switch (A+, this design), and hybrid (B). A+ captures ~90% of B’s user value — triage, response latency (within one instant switch), multi-device coherence, teardown, reconnect — and is strictly better on wrong-host safety: this persona’s dominant shape is the same repo cloned on two machines, where two tiles named kolu · master · ~/code/kolu differ only by a badge, and the dock (repo-keyed, no host axis) would interleave them. When the PWA is backgrounded — most of the day — B’s on-canvas pips are invisible anyway; both models converge on the OS notification. What only B gives: live side-by-side of two hosts’ terminals in one window. That is deferred, demand-driven (W4), and stays additive: the daemons are host-complete, so B is an aggregation layer over N bindings touching only kolu-server + client. The first escalation if switching proves insufficient is not B but the cross-host dock (W4): foreign hosts’ rows rendered in the dock, click = switch + focus — attention wants a cross-host dock, not a cross-host canvas. The dock projection carries an optional host axis from W1 so that lands without a contract break.

Architecture-level changes

The volatility, named. kaval serves PTY; pulam serves awareness + fs/git by re-dialing kaval; and the authored record, memory, restore target, session persistence, sleep/wake and boot adoption live only inside kolu-server — nothing serves the complete terminal state of one host from one place. That gap is why the old plan had to thread HostLocation per-terminal through ~11 dispatch sites behind two parallel seams (resolveTerminalEndpoint + localOnly) with two discriminators (hostId for daemon connections, HostLocation for records) — N-site handling of a 1-seam volatility. Meanwhile the client is already single-host by construction: one websocket to one origin, every RPC keyed by id/repoPath, location read by zero non-test code. padi closes the gap at the right altitude: host-complete daemon below, connection-scoped binding above, nothing per-terminal anywhere.

The three layers, and what each owns

layer owns explicitly does NOT own
kaval (per padi) PTYs, screen mirror, VT taps, inventory — unchanged role, byte-minimal; the overflow frame shipped (#1591, contract 5.0) awareness, git, sleep, persistence (as today)
padi (per host × state-root) the registry (terminals = authored ⋈ snapshot, composed server-side); the one fold + AgentMemory on the host’s clock; restoreTarget; lifecycle (create · kill · sleep/wake · boot adoption · inventory reconcile); spawn-policy composition against its kaval’s system.info; fs/git endpoint + watcher pulses; byte procedures (previewRead · scratch.write); transcript.exportHtml (the agent loaders run host-side — codex/opencode transcripts are SQLite queries, not file reads); worktree create/remove; the MRU activity feed (recentRepos/recentAgents — host-fs facts written inside the fold’s emit path); session persistence in its state-root; kaval supervision; status cells (kaval daemonStatus, expectedKaval); the urgency projection (awaiting counts + ids — no recency); the frozen control core (hello · version · drain · clock.now) serving browsers; user preferences; which client looks at it
kolu-server HTTP · static · PWA · websocket; user-scoped preferences; buildInfo (minus expectedKaval, which moves to padi); the binding pool (dial + supervise-or-front padis; per-connection scope; re-serve); binding/link status on its own shell surface; notification fan-in across bindings terminals — it writes nothing about them. No registry, no fold, no adoption, no terminal-derived cells — with one read-only exception: the cross-binding urgency sum for badges, sourced solely from each padi’s urgency-projection member
client one padiSurface consumer per view + the shell surface; ages recency against the bind-time clock offset (never the browser clock vs a foreign clock) per-terminal host routing — inexpressible; HostLocation is deleted from records and create input

padi’s identity is the state-root, and binders never recycle. kaval is deliberately namespaced per kolu-server listen port (kaval-<port>, the #1313 prod-incident fix) because its supervisor recycles on skew. padi inverts the relationship: one padi per (host, state-root). Prod binds the host’s default state-root; a dev kolu-server and each e2e worker pass a private state-root — which brings its own private kaval. So “dev recycles prod” is unspellable, and “a second kolu sees the same arrangement” is true by construction — both, because isolation-vs-sharing became an explicit axis instead of a port accident. The mechanics, each pinned by a round-2 adversarial finding:

  • Identity is persistent; the rendezvous is not. The state-root is persistent storage (it must survive host reboots — the restore flows depend on it). The socket + atomic pid-gate live in the runtime dir, keyed by a digest of the state-root path ($XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/padi-<digest>/, and kaval-<digest>/ for padi’s kaval) — tmpfs auto-invalidates gates at boot, so a recycled PID can never read as a live holder (kill(pid,0)→EPERM wedging every bind), and the 108-byte sun_path limit can’t bite an arbitrarily-deep state-root. A small manifest in each runtime dir (digest → state-root) keeps kaval-tui’s flag-less discovery working: discoverKavalCandidates scans the kaval-<digest> pattern and labels candidates honestly — discovery + labels are a named W2 deliverable, not a release-note casualty.
  • The default state-root is spelled by the padi binary, on the host — a fixed derivation in one place (the getRuntimeSocketPath discipline), never re-derived by a binder or transmitted over the wire, so two entry paths (login shell vs ssh non-interactive env) cannot silently mint two “defaults”. Binders pass nothing (default) or an explicit absolute root (dev/e2e).
  • Binders hold adopt-or-spawn right, never recycle right — a new, third supervisor policy (the existing endpoint ships only always-recycle and adopt-or-recycle-on-skew; W2 names adopt-or-spawn-or-refuse explicitly so an implementer doesn’t reach for the recycling one). The empty-gate race is already safe (link(2)-atomic acquisition; the loser exits clean and dials the winner).
  • Version convergence is monotonic, through a frozen control core. padiSurface grows; a versioned handshake alone would dead-end (a contract-revving deploy makes padi.restart unreachable inside the very contract that refused). So padi serves a tiny version-agnostic control core — hello/identity/version/drain — frozen forever, beside padiSurface. Rule: a binder older than the running padi gets the typed skew refusal (upgrade your kolu — loud, per #1034); a binder newer may invoke control-core drain (padi persists state and exits; PTYs survive in kaval) and spawn its own newer closure. Newest-wins is monotonic: two binders at different versions converge instead of livelocking, and no path ever kill-9s a padi.

The state model — what survives from the awareness design, and what moves

The observe-vs-remember cut survives intact inside padi: startSensors stays the memoryless producer (it still cannot spell memory, by type), fold stays the one fold, TerminalSnapshot/AgentMemory/Known<T>/RestoreTarget are reused verbatim. What moves is the home of remembering: producer and fold are now co-resident in padi, so no fold crosses a wire — the framer / seq/gap frames / consumer-arm apparatus (#1638) is deleted unbuilt, and the #1614 fold-clobber class loses its second writer (kolu-server holds no competing copy; it re-serves). Two consequences are re-settled deliberately, not silently:

  • Owner-clock replaces consumer-clock. Memory is stamped by the host’s clock. Within one canvas that clock is the only one — cross-host recency comparison is unrepresentable because no view ever holds two padis’ terminals. For display (“2m ago”, staleness buckets), the client ages against a clock offset measured once per bind — a control-core clock.now() exchange, RTT-halved, re-measured on rebind — so a skewed host cannot mis-bucket its own terminals against the browser’s clock. (Deliberately not a served ticking now cell: a cell replays its last written value — stale by padi’s uptime at bind — and ticking one is a permanent frame across every warm binding, the tick-push class the persistence path already engineered out.) Anything cross-host (switcher badges, the W4 cross-host dock) is urgency-only — counts and awaiting-states, no recency on any multi-host wire — keeping the settled dashboards-are-urgency-only rule.
  • The reader-join retires. R8’s authored ⋈ snapshots client join existed because two processes wrote the two halves. In padi one process writes both, so padi serves a single composed terminals collection (the compose is composeTerminalMetadata, moved server-side; disk persistence keeps using the same compose). The authored/snapshot split remains as padi-internal types — the fence that keeps the producer unable to write memory — it just stops being a wire shape.

Session state and restore — the host restores itself

Today, session state lives in kolu-server’s conf-store and restore is client-driven: after a server restart the browser’s useSessionRestore loop re-creates saved terminals one create RPC at a time (with a known respawn-vs-surviving-PTY race the old plan had to design around). Under padi, session state moves to the entity that can actually guarantee it (P5), and restore stops involving the client entirely:

state lives survives
live PTYs + scrollback kaval (RAM) padi restarts, kolu-server restarts, ssh blips
terminal session — authored records (layout · chrome · active tile · panel sizes · memory · restoreTarget) + the restore-relevant awareness projection padi’s state-root, on the host (persistent storage — pinned) host reboots included — at a reboot only the PTYs die (kaval’s row); the records park for restore
user preferences · binding pool (which hosts, last binding per view) kolu-server conf-store kolu-server has no terminal state to lose
per-tab view posture (canvas-maximized, minimap-expanded, …) browser localStorage, namespaced per binding host switches don’t collide two hosts’ view-state. (Active tile + panel sizes are NOT here — they ride padi’s authored session via the chrome procedures, as today’s setActive/setSubPanel persistence does)

The four restore flows, each owned by the layer that knows:

  1. padi restarts (deploy, crash): on boot it adopts surviving kaval PTYs by id, reconciles them against its persisted session, re-seeds fold memory from its store, and re-runs sensors fresh (the #1031 posture). By the time any client binds, the canvas is already whole — no client participates.
  2. Host reboots (kaval’s PTYs are gone): padi’s boot reconcile finds the saved records with no live PTYs and parks them under a distinct parked record state — deliberately-slept records stay sleeping and are not touched (waking them resumes their agents; parking must not erase that distinction — the two arms restore oppositely, exactly as today’s restore treats active vs sleeping records). padi never auto-respawns agents with nobody watching, and a warm pool binding is not a watcher — so restore is never a bind side-effect: the browser renders today’s restore card over the served parked state and the user’s click calls session.restore({resumeIds}) (per-terminal agent-resume opt-out preserved). The respawn+resume then executes inside padi as one writer, idempotent (the parked→active flip per record is the idempotency token, so two simultaneous binds serialize harmlessly) — the client-loop respawn race is structurally gone. “Import session” likewise becomes a padi procedure.
  3. kolu-server restarts: nothing happens to terminals at all — padi’s registry stays warm (strictly better than today, where every restart re-derives all metadata). The browser reconnects and rebinds; the canvas is byte-identical.
  4. Browser reload / device switch: pure re-subscribe. The client no longer contains respawn logic of any kind; a reload against a rebooted remote host lands in flow 2 on that host’s padi, same as local.

padiSurface — one contract, with per-member forwarding semantics

padi serves a new surface, padiSurface 1.0 (terminalWorkspaceSurface 3.0 is frozen and dies with its pulam-era consumers in W0/W2 — growing it would force three packages slated for deletion to implement every new member in lockstep, since implementSurface fail-fast throws on any unimplemented primitive). Members: version + status cells; the composed terminals collection (record states active | sleeping | parked; the dock projection carries the optional host axis, reserved); the urgency projection (awaiting counts + terminal ids, recency-free — the sole thing kolu-server reads from every warm binding, for badge fan-in); activity stream; repo/file {seq} pulses; fs/git procedures (incl. worktree ops); byte procedures; transcript.exportHtml; lifecycle + chrome procedures; session.restore / session.import; terminalExit event; terminalAttach. Beside the surface, the frozen control core (hello · version · drain · clock.now) that never versions.

Per-connection binding scope is new machinery, named as such. Today the surface framework binds every member’s handlers to one store at build time, and kolu-server upgrades every websocket into one module-level router — nothing is per-connection. W2/W3 build the scope: per-binding mirror stores (shared by all connections parked on the same host) + router instantiation keyed by the connection’s declared binding, and the client’s module-level wire singletons (wire.ts, useTerminalStore) become binding-scoped so an in-app switch rebuilds them against the new scope (v1 of the switch may be a reload — the fused-lifecycle degenerate case — but the wire scoping is designed in from the start). Whatever lands in @kolu/surface* rides the same drishti gate as the forwarding helpers.

The contract’s one structural novelty, forced by the adversarial review: members are typed by forwarding policy. Value-bearing members (cells, collections, pulses) may be mirrored hold-open-and-rebind — a rebind just replays current values. Delta-bearing streams (terminalAttach, activity) are fail-through: a mid-chain disconnect (padi↔kolu-server) MUST terminate the downstream browser stream, so the client’s existing retry re-attaches end-to-end and a scrollback snapshot is only ever the first frame of a fresh stream — a hold-open forwarder splicing a replayed snapshot into a live xterm as bytes becomes a type error in the re-serve helper, not a convention. Attach thus keeps today’s deliberate carve-out (a dedicated per-subscriber stream, 1:1 through each hop: browser→kolu-server→padi→kaval), and the shipped overflow frame (#1591) rides it unchanged. The forwarding helpers this needs (per-member policy; input-keyed stream forward) graduate from pulam-web’s reserve into the surface stack — the one piece of genuine electricity this plan mints (domain-agnostic, real transport volatility, second consumer = kolu-server itself); that change is drishti-gated as usual.

Rejected shapes (each killed by a specific review finding)

  • Per-terminal HostLocation threading (the finale plan): N-site handling of a connection-level volatility; the client never needed it. Closed with #1637–#1640.
  • Merging kaval into padi: padi bundles the system’s highest-churn code (agent sensors, fs/git serving); kaval earns “restarts only on contract change” by containing nothing that churns. Fusing them means sensor churn kills PTYs. padi restarts are instead cheap by design: re-seed from the state-root + fresh sensors (the #1031 posture — warm across web-shell churn, honestly re-derived across padi churn).
  • Server-global binding with a “rebind + client reset” switch (the brief’s own first draft): behind a constant origin, in-flight id/repoPath-keyed calls and every other device silently straddle a rebind — a two-actor temporal convention with a race window. Replaced by per-connection binding scope: a call minted under one binding cannot reach another, and other devices are untouched by your switch.
  • Local padi as in-process library, process only for remote: keeps two assemblies (the drift the one-library-two-homes era already suffered), keeps kolu-server fat, and forfeits warm-across-restart metadata and multi-client (kolu-tui) for the local host.

Implementation details

W0· decks cleared — freeze the old contract, retire pulam-web, land the overflow frame, close the PRs○ todo
W1· the padi seam, in place — padiSurface served in-process, client migrated, root oRPC dispositioned○ todo
needs ← W0
W2· padi the process — state-root identity, owns kaval, on-host persistence; kolu-server binds + re-serves○ todo
needs ← W1
W3· remote + the switch — ssh/Nix leg, host picker, per-view bindings, cross-host badges○ todo
needs ← W2
W4· demand-driven — cross-host dock (B-lite) · kolu-tui · hybrid canvas only if earned○ todo
needs ← W3

W0 — decks cleared (all three landed)

  1. Freeze terminalWorkspaceSurface at 3.0 + retire pulam-web — ✓ shipped (#1650). No member is ever added to the frozen surface (this alone breaks the lockstep: padiSurface is new, so nothing forces members through the dying consumers); pulam-web goes with its flake/nix wiring in the same change. pulam-tui and the pulam daemon survive until W2: pulam-tui wait is the agent-drives-agent done-signal (the /kolu and /debate skills, the dock-fleet-mirror rule, and the home-manager module all depend on it), so they retire only when W2 re-homes that signal.
  2. The overflow frame — ✓ shipped (#1591, kaval contract 5.0): kaval’s attach output carries the typed overflow → re-attach signal, and the recovery loop (reattachingDeltas.ts) re-attaches with a fresh snapshot, distinguishable from PTY exit. The loop landed in kolu-server’s endpoint — it migrates into padi with the domain at W2, and W1.4 re-runs its e2e through padiSurface.terminalAttach.
  3. Close #1637 #1638 #1639 #1640 — ✓ done (2026-07-01, each with a pointer here); the finale is marked superseded. #1577 (kaval transcript memory) is orthogonal and unaffected.

W1 — the padi seam, in place (strangler; eight PRs, phased below; UX byte-identical throughout)

Define padiSurface 1.0 and serve it in-process from kolu-server’s existing terminal domain; migrate the client member-by-member. The phase exists to prove one path — the client runs entirely on padiSurface + the shell surface, with the in-process server behind the same contract a remote padi will serve — so its terminal done-criterion is the import-seal test (W1.8). Every PR lands green with the UX byte-identical; W1.1 opens the fan, W1.2–W1.6 are parallel, W1.7 needs W1.2 + W1.3, W1.8 closes. The root oRPC namespace dies entirely across them, per-member (each row names its PR):

root oRPC member disposition PR
terminal.create/kill/killAll
terminal.sleep/wake
terminal.discardSleeping/restoreSleeping
terminal.setTheme/setIntent/setParent/setActive
terminal.setCanvasLayout/setSubPanel/setRightPanel
terminal.resize/sendInput		 padiSurface lifecycle + chrome procedures (create loses the client-supplied lastActivityAt — padi stamps) W1.3
terminal.attach padiSurface.terminalAttach — the fail-through per-subscriber stream (the carve-out, kept) W1.4
terminal.screenState/screenText padiSurface procedures W1.4
terminal.pasteImage/uploadFile scratch.write + a paste procedure (bytes land on the terminal’s host) W1.6
terminal.exportTranscriptHtml transcript.exportHtml({terminalId, mode}) → {html, filename} — loaders (claude JSONL, codex/opencode SQLite) run host-side W1.6
git.worktreeCreate/worktreeRemove padiSurface git procedures (they are the host’s fs volatility — a worktree materializing on the wrong machine becomes unspellable) W1.6
the client-driven restore loop (useSessionRestore respawns) deleted — padi’s boot reconcile + session.restore/session.import (see the restore section) W1.7
daemon.restart becomes a binding procedure — restart the bound padi (drain-and-exit; PTYs survive) — when the binding exists W2
server.info stays — web shell —

W1.1 — padiSurface 1.0 + the control core, served in-process (opens the fan)

The contract: every member from the surface section — the composed terminals collection (with parked in the record-state union and the host axis reserved on the dock projection), the urgency projection, activity, the repo/file pulses, fs/git + worktree + byte procedures, transcript.exportHtml, lifecycle + chrome procedures, session.restore/session.import, terminalExit, terminalAttach — each annotated with its forwarding policy (value vs fail-through), plus the frozen control-core types (hello · version · drain · clock.now). Served complete from kolu-server in-process (implementSurface is fail-fast, so no member may stub; every backing exists in today’s domain code — registry projections, the terminal-workspace endpoint, the scratch/transcript/worktree helpers, an urgency fold off the registry). Zero client consumers yet.

  • Done: the surface serves beside koluSurface; a contract test pins the member list, the policy annotations, and version 1.0; all e2e untouched.

W1.2 — the composed terminals collection (∥ after W1.1)

useTerminalMetadata reads the one terminals collection; composeTerminalMetadata moves server-side (still the single join shared with disk-save, so disk and the read can’t diverge); the client-side join and its two collection subscriptions (authored, snapshots) are deleted.

  • Done: a differential test pins the client store byte-identical pre/post; no client reference to the halves survives; Dock/StatePip/recency render unchanged.

W1.3 — lifecycle + chrome procedures (∥ after W1.1)

The sixteen terminal.* lifecycle/chrome RPCs move onto padiSurface procedures per the table; create stops accepting lastActivityAt (padi stamps with its clock); the root members are deleted as each call site migrates.

  • Done: crud/sleep-wake/theme e2e green; contract.ts retains no migrated member.

W1.4 — terminalAttach + screen procedures (∥ after W1.1)

The tile’s attach swaps from the root unenrolledStreamCall onto padiSurface.terminalAttach — same per-subscriber fail-through semantics, snapshot only ever the first frame of a fresh stream; screenState/screenText become procedures.

  • Done: the R5 slow-subscriber e2e (#1591) re-runs green through the surface member — the overflow frame passes intact; typing-echo latency measured as the W2 baseline.

W1.5 — Code tab on procedure + pulse (∥ after W1.1)

Re-derive #1639’s client half: createPolledQuery (procedure + {seq} pulse → subscription with .pending()), one repoPulse driving git status/diff/file-list, the #818 selection-stability guard, binary-preview/iframe orchestration client-side. koluSurface’s gitStatus/gitDiff/fsListAll/fsReadFile value streams are deleted.

  • Done: Code tab byte-identical incl. preview + the selection guard; the four streams gone from koluSurface.

W1.6 — bytes + host-fs procedures (∥ after W1.1)

The iframe preview byte route reads via previewRead (dropping the raw @kolu/serve-dir bypass); paste/upload via scratch.write + the paste procedure; transcript export via transcript.exportHtml (the three agent loaders — claude JSONL, codex/opencode SQLite — behind it); worktree create/remove via the git procedures.

  • Done: preview (incl. the ../%2f/symlink 403s, re-enforced at the endpoint), paste/upload, transcript export for all three agents, and the worktree flow e2e green; their root oRPC members deleted.

W1.7 — session: parked + restore procedures (needs W1.2 + W1.3)

The parked record state lands; boot reconcile parks reboot-killed actives server-side (sleeping records untouched); the restore card calls session.restore({resumeIds}) (consent + per-terminal resume opt-out preserved); session.import replaces the import flow; useSessionRestore’s respawn loop is deleted.

  • Done: the reload-restore e2e passes via the card; a test asserts the client issues zero create calls during restore; deliberately-slept records stay dormant.

W1.8 — MRU + status relocation, and the import-seal (last)

trackRecentRepo/trackRecentAgent and the MRU feed move into the padi domain module (served on padiSurface; validated against the domain’s own fs), expectedKaval moves onto padi’s status cell — kolu-server’s surface retains no terminal-derived cell. Then the seal: an import test (à la #1640’s sealedDispatch, re-derived) pins that client-facing serving reaches the terminal domain only through the padiSurface implementation, and that the root terminal.* namespace is empty.

  • Done: the seal test is green and fails on any reintroduced bypass import; W1 exits with the domain module extractable — W2 moves it out of process without touching the contract.

W2 — padi the process (local; four PRs, phased below; UX byte-identical; restarts get warm)

W1.8’s seal guarantees the domain module is extractable behind the contract; W2 moves it out of process. W2.1 is framework work and can run in parallel with all of W1; W2.2 needs W1.8; W2.3 needs both; W2.4 buries the pulam era.

W2.1 — the re-serve machinery in @kolu/surface* (∥ with W1; drishti-gated)

Graduate pulam-web’s forwarding shapes into the surface stack, generalized: per-member forwarding policy (value members hold-open-and-rebind; delta streams fail-through — the helper refuses to hold-open an undeclared stream, making the snapshot-spliced-as-bytes corruption a type error), the input-keyed stream forward (today only pulam-web’s app-local forwardInputStream, whose hold-open contract is wrong for attach), and per-binding mirror stores + binding-keyed router instantiation (today walkSurface binds handlers to one store at build time and every websocket shares one module-level router — nothing is per-connection).

  • Done: unit-proven against a toy surface (kill-the-middle-hop on a fail-through member terminates downstream; a hold-open member replays values); the paired drishti PR passes CI pinned to final kolu HEAD per .claude/rules/surface.md.

W2.2 — the padi binary (needs W1.8; zero kolu consumers, like W1.1)

packages/padi: the sealed domain module gets its process entry — package = process = staleKey (kaval’s one rule; its own Nix derivation + closure). Identity per the architecture section: persistent state-root with the default spelled by the padi binary on the host; digest-keyed runtime rendezvous (padi-<digest>/, gates auto-invalidate at boot); 0700 socket; the frozen control core (hello · version · drain · clock.now) served for real. padi spawns-or-adopts its kaval under kaval-<digest>/ (retiring kaval-<port>), and kaval-tui’s discovery + labels move to the digest pattern + a manifest (digest → state-root) in the same PR — a flag-less kaval-tui keeps working throughout. Session persistence lands in the state-root. Nothing in kolu-server consumes it yet — the daemon is proven standalone.

  • Done: a dial test (kaval-tui-style) spawns-or-adopts padi at a private state-root, handshakes the control core, round-trips a terminal through padiSurface over the socket; two padis at distinct state-roots never touch each other’s kaval (the #1313 property, by construction); nix build produces the padi closure with a stable staleKey.

W2.3 — kolu-server binds (needs W2.1 + W2.2; the cutover)

kolu-server’s in-process serving is deleted; it binds the local padi (adopt-or-spawn-or-refuse — a new supervisor policy; the existing endpoint’s skew arm recycles and must not be reused) and re-serves through W2.1’s machinery with the typed forwarding policy. Migration: padi’s boot imports kolu-server’s saved-session file once if its store is empty, then hard cutover (SCHEMA_VERSION bump, no fallback — a failed import crashes loudly). Boot adoption, inventory reconcile, the MRU feed, and the R5 recovery loop (reattachingDeltas) now run inside padi. The e2e harness gives each cucumber worker a private state-root (its sandboxed $HOME exists; the agent-dir env vars flow into padi’s spawn).

  • Done, each a test: (a) UX byte-identical, all e2e green; (b) kolu-server restart keeps metadata warm (the registry survives in padi); (c) kill-the-middle-hop: killing padi mid-attach terminates the browser stream and the retry re-attaches with a fresh snapshot — never spliced bytes; killing kolu-server and rebinding adopts, never respawns; (d) the fold differential pins local awareness byte-identical pre/post extraction; (e) typing-echo p99 within budget of the W1.4 baseline (< 5ms added).

W2.4 — burial (needs W2.3)

pulam-tui and the pulam daemon retire, with the wait/status done-signal re-homed onto padi first (a thin padi-fed wait, or kaval-tui growing it) — the agent-drives-agent workflow never loses its precise done-signal. The home-manager module, flake default, the /kolu//debate skills, and the dock-fleet-mirror rule update in the same change. And the frozen terminalWorkspaceSurface is deleted — its last servers are gone; freezing (#1650) was the tourniquet, this is the burial. @kolu/terminal-workspace survives as the library padi assembles (sensors · fold · schema · endpoint · agentProjection), minus its surface.

  • Done: no package serves or consumes terminalWorkspaceSurface (the export is gone); padi wait (or equivalent) drives the /kolu skill’s done-signal in a live loop; flake eval + home-manager module green.

W3 — remote + the switch (three PRs, phased below; the user-visible feature)

W3.1 — the remote binding (needs W2.3; complete but invisible)

The named path this PR exists to prove: padiSurface consumed over ssh. Arch-keyed drv maps for padi (kaval rides inside padi’s closure reference — one provisionAgent dial provisions both); getHostSession + frontDaemonOverStdio front the remote padi exactly as kaval-tui fronts kaval; blip-survival from HostSession reconnect + the durable daemon; the remote binding is reachable via a config/env knob only — no UI yet.

  • Done: the remote-canvas parity checklist e2e against a real ssh host (a pu box): PTY echo · Code tab git/diff/tree · binary preview · paste/upload · transcript export (all three agent kinds) · sleep/wake · worktree flow · kolu-server restart re-adopt · browser reload re-dial · host-reboot park-and-restore — every axis, plus all local e2e green. The done-criterion can only be met by driving the ssh leg — no local shortcut satisfies it.

W3.2 — the switch (needs W3.1)

Binding pool in kolu-server; per-connection binding declared in the wire scope; the client’s wire singletons rebuilt per binding (the in-app instant switch — W2.1 laid the server-side scope); the clock offset measured at each bind (control-core clock.now(), RTT-halved); per-binding localStorage namespace for view posture; ChromeBar host picker (ssh-config sourced + free-text); persisted last-binding per view; #1034 loud connecting/degraded/skew states; create refused while the binding warms (today’s refuseIfWarming, per binding).

  • Done: an e2e switches local → remote → local with tiles live on both ends — no cross-binding call survives a switch (a stale in-flight id-keyed call is rejected, not misrouted); two browser views parked on different hosts stay independent; the switch never disturbs another device.

W3.3 — cross-host attention (needs W3.2; the A+ bar from the UX evaluation)

kolu-server subscribes each warm binding’s urgency-projection member (the one thing it reads from every pool host) and sums it onto the shell surface: PWA app badge aggregated across bindings; per-host switcher-chip counts; OS notification whose click = switch + focus (1 action). Urgency-only on any multi-host wire — no recency, no snapshots.

  • Done: an agent flips to awaiting on host B while every view is parked on host A → chip count, app badge, and notification all fire; the notification click lands focused on the right tile; killing host B’s link degrades its chip, never the app.

W4 — demand-driven

Cross-host dock (B-lite: foreign hosts’ rows, urgency-ranked, click = switch + focus — the host axis is already in the contract) is the first escalation if switching proves insufficient. kolu-tui — the graduation proof: consume padiSurface over the socket/ssh without kolu-server; done only when that named path renders a live canvas (the path, not the feature, is the unit of done). Hybrid canvas (model B) only if one-window side-by-side proves a recurring demand — it stays an aggregation layer over N bindings, zero daemon changes.

Risks, named, with mitigations

  • Attach latency gains a local hop (kaval→padi→kolu-server vs kaval→kolu-server). Measure typing-echo p99 before/after W2 with a budget (< 5ms added); if breached, the carve-out permits a raw-byte relay on the padi→kolu-server leg (frontDaemonOverStdio-style, no decode) without touching the contract.
  • Sensor churn forces padi restarts (padi hashes its whole package). Accepted: a padi restart re-seeds from the state-root + re-runs sensors (seconds, PTYs untouched). If deploy cadence makes this hurt, the sensors split into a padi-hosted, non-hashed volatility — an observed decision, not a pre-paid one.
  • Multi-client writes (two kolu-servers, one padi): padi serializes all chrome/layout procedures — one writer, last-write-wins, concurrent editors see each other’s moves live. Accepted semantics (same as any shared session).
  • drishti gate: the graduated forwarding helpers (per-member policy, input-keyed forward) and any per-connection scope machinery landing in @kolu/surface* — paired drishti PR, pinned to final kolu HEAD, per .claude/rules/surface.md.
  • N warm bindings have a standing cost (one ssh session + one urgency subscription per host, even while parked elsewhere). Bounded: the pool holds only user-added hosts, the subscription is the count-sized urgency member (never terminals), and an unreachable host degrades its chip, not the app. Costed explicitly rather than discovered in production.
  • Session-file migration: one-shot import at padi’s first boot, then hard cutover — a failed import crashes loudly (fail-fast), never silently starts empty.

Superseded by this note

The finale (whole plan: PR-1/2/3, F-REMOTE, R10) — closed PRs #1637, #1638, #1639, #1640. Partially superseded: awareness-derive-store (the fold’s home moves to padi; owner-clock replaces consumer-clock; producer, fold, and types survive verbatim) and the terminal model (the reader-join collapses into padi’s composed terminals collection; the authored/snapshot split stays as padi-internal types). Unaffected: kaval’s design (pty-daemon) apart from the shipped overflow frame (contract 5.0, #1591); #1577.

Referenced by

  • Awareness — the producer observes, kolu remembers
  • Remote terminals — the finale (R9–R10)
  • Remote terminals over SSH
  • The Terminal Model — two collections, one reader-side join (and what to name them)
← the Atlasgithub ↗
